title: Vomit on Carpet
stainChemistry: Vomit is a complex mixture of partially digested food, stomach acid (HCl, pH 1.5-3.5), bile salts, mucus, and proteins. On carpet, the acid can damage dyes and fibers if left in contact, while the protein component bonds and sets with heat. Bile salts act as surfactants that drive the stain deeper into the substrate. The odor comes from volatile fatty acids and bacterial decomposition. pH-neutral detergents are essential to avoid further damage to the carpet from acidic or alkaline residues.
whyThisWorks: Immediate removal of solids prevents acid damage to carpet. Cold water dilutes the acid and prevents protein from setting. Enzyme treatment breaks down the protein matrix, while surfactant addresses the bile and fat components. Odor neutralization requires reaching all contaminated material beneath the surface.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Protein Formula (digestant spotter) and tamp gently with spotting brush. Work from edge to center
2. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and work gently to remove loosened protein and digestant residue. Flush with cold water
3. If stain persists: apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) for stubborn oxidized protein — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor carefully
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse to restore fabric pH
5. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth. Air dry — no heat
warnings: COLD water only throughout — heat permanently sets protein
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing/foam damage

homeSolutions.agent: Protect yourself — vomit is a biohazard
homeSolutions.instruction: Before you touch anything: wear disposable gloves and, if the smell is strong or the affected person was sick with a stomach bug, an N95 or surgical mask. Norovirus and rotavirus spread from vomit for hours after the fact. Open a window. Use dedicated cleanup towels you can throw away — don't reuse them on other rooms. Keep kids and pets out of the area until fully cleaned and dry.

homeSolutions.agent: Lift solids — don't rub, don't flush them down
homeSolutions.instruction: Scoop solids off the carpet with a stiff piece of cardboard, a paper plate, or a dustpan. Work from the outside edge toward the center so you don't spread the spill. Drop everything straight into a plastic bag, tie it off, and put it outside. Don't wipe, don't rub, and don't rinse solids down the carpet — that pushes acid and food pigment deeper into the fiber and the pad.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold water dilute — neutralize the acid fast
homeSolutions.instruction: Vomit has stomach acid (pH around 2) which can damage carpet dye if it sits. Pour a small amount of cold water over the area and immediately blot with a stack of white paper towels or a white cotton towel. Repeat three or four times. Do NOT use hot water — heat sets the protein. Cold water dilutes the acid and flushes food pigment before it can bond.

Mix one tablespoon of clear Dawn dish soap with two cups of cold water. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, and dab the area. Let it sit 5 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Dawn cuts bile salts and fats that vinegar alone can't touch, and it primes the carpet for the enzyme step that actually handles the protein. Do not use OxiClean or baking soda — alkaline products set protein.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme cleaner — saturate the contaminated area
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply a pet-stain enzyme cleaner (Nature's Miracle, Rocco & Roxie, Simple Solution) even though it's not a pet accident — these are the strongest consumer-grade enzymes, and vomit is chemically similar to the stains they're built for. Saturate enough to reach the pad, not just the surface. The enzyme digests the protein and the food residue. This is the step that actually eliminates odor, not vinegar.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Enzymes are deactivated by chlorine bleach, ammonia, hot water, and strong alkaline cleaners. Don't mix with any of them.

homeSolutions.instruction: Lay a damp (not dripping) white towel over the treated area and cover with plastic wrap. Tape the edges. This keeps the enzyme working at full moisture instead of drying out on the surface. Leave 30–60 minutes for fresh, several hours for older. Uncover, blot any surface moisture with clean paper towels.

If a sour odor lingers even after the enzyme dwell, mix one tablespoon of white vinegar into one cup of cold water and dab onto the area with a white cloth. Vinegar neutralizes residual odor molecules the enzyme left behind. Blot dry. Skip baking soda entirely — it's alkaline and will reactivate protein bonds you just broke down.

homeSolutions.agent: Dry down + smell and stain check
homeSolutions.instruction: Stack six to ten sheets of white paper towels over the area, weigh with a heavy book, leave overnight to pull moisture out of the pad. In the morning, inspect in daylight and put your nose close to the carpet. If you smell anything or see a shadow, repeat steps 5–6 once more. Once clean and dry, vacuum the area to restore pile.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate — especially for food-pigment halos or lingering odor
homeSolutions.instruction: If a food-color halo (tomato, berry, wine) remains after two enzyme rounds, or if odor persists, the pad is likely contaminated. Tell a pro: "Vomit on [nylon/polyester/wool] carpet, treated with Dawn + enzyme (2 rounds), vinegar rinse. No hot water, no ammonia, no bleach used. Residual [pigment halo/odor] remains — suspect pad contamination." Pros have sub-surface extraction and tannin-specific chemistry to finish the job.

steps: 1. Remove solids with a spoon or dull edge — scrape, don't rub. Dispose safely
2. Blot area with cold water and clean white cloths to dilute acid
3. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 2 cups cold water. Apply and blot
4. Mix equal parts white vinegar and cold water. Apply for odor neutralization
5. Blot with clean white cloth until dry. Sprinkle baking soda over area — let sit 15 min
6. Vacuum and air dry — no heat
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop and take to a professional if the stain has been heat-set, if multiple home treatments haven't improved it, or if the fabric is delicate/expensive
warnings: NEVER use hot water on protein stains — heat sets them permanently
Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first

neverDo: No chlorine bleach on colored carpet
Avoid over-wetting — causes backing damage and mold
fiberSensitivities: Most synthetic carpet (nylon/polyester) tolerates standard chemistry
Always blot from outside in to prevent spreading
Extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

escalation.when: If the protein has been heat-set (hot water, dryer, or iron exposure), or if the stain is older than 48 hours without treatment, or if 3 treatment cycles haven't resolved it
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is vomit on carpet, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaning service with truck-mount extraction

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the stain fresh and wet, or dried/crusty?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
